kept.text:
Recently, @bmsnews utilized the Tempus Next Pathways program to address care gaps patients with advanced non-small cell lung cancer (aNSCLC). 

Deployed across 13 community-based health systems, the Tempus Next Pathways program provided a unified, longitudinal view of the patient journey, delivering actionable insights that helped close 41% of the biomarker testing care gap.

Learn more about this work and how Tempus can help unlock insights and accelerate your programs here: https://t.co/0Wuc9CPOgv

kept.text:
Just sharing for fun. $TEM Tempus AI looks like an interesting company. 

My very basic understanding is they work upstream in the drug discovery process… way upstream.. target discovery. And they have a massive amount of wet-lab data from sequencing tumors. I believe they help identify ideal drug targets for the drug discovery companies to go off of downstream… fascinating. 

Founder led. Strong revenue growth, with over 80% YoY as of recent. Diluting tf out of shareholders though. Stock is around 8x sales though which isn’t terrible. 64% gross margin and growing is pretty solid. 

Grok told me their moat is their massive amount of in-house wet-lab data they generate.. All I did was ask grok so don’t hold me to that, but if that’s the case.. that’s interesting. I believe in-house generated functional wet-lab data is superior to buying 3rd party data. Grok guessed that around 5% of their total data is functional. Hm. I know through learning about antibody discovery that the data being ~functional~ is key.. although idk what that means in the context of target discovery. I would assume the same applies here. I’ll look into it. 

Sequencing the tumors doesn’t have the same obstacles as antibodies, for example; so it seems the primary barrier to entry they have is just being an early mover with data flywheel, vs special capabilities built out for the sequencing itself. Network effects perhaps.

I’m sure my understanding isn’t perfect since I just started looking at it and there’s probably a lot more to it.. but it’s pretty interesting. I’m familiar with drug discovery, but not target discovery. Cool. I’m already heavy in one biotech though, which is risky enough as-is, so I’m not going to get distracted and get into a second one right now.. but I find this stuff really fun to learn about. I might keep looking into this one slowly over time just because it seems so interesting. 

To be clear, I’m not making a bull case here.. I barely know anything about them. They made some headline in Time magazine and I took a look and thought it was cool. Figured some might enjoy, so I’m throwing it out there.

kept.text:
I started getting really excited about AI last year.  

I actually recommended a somewhat speculative AI name (which of course we stopped out of), Tempus AI, which I believe is building something truly valuable. 

The more I looked for more AI-related stocks, the more I found a highly financialized world colliding with the physical world. 

You start with seemingly unlimited capital -- lent into existence by the world's biggest Cantillon effect beneficiaries -- which is headed for a data center. 

Yay innovation and wealth creation, right?

Well, sort of... The capital will ultimately have to find the real bottleneck, which is not people who want to build data centers. 

Data centers are less powered by GPUs than by natural gas, coal, uranium and diesel backup generators. 

The US has more than 5,400 data centers (according to Cloudscene https://t.co/cIsUr69bQ8) and 95% of them use diesel backups. 

If the power goes out for a day or so anyplace where there's a lot of data centers, diesel delivery trucks will clog the roads.  

So what you really find is a Cantillon effect beneficiary at one end and an oil refinery at the other. 

Maybe you figure everybody already knows the bottleneck is energy, so the trade is done... but that's not what you see when you look at independent drillers, refineries, pipelines, chemical companies and other critical energy infrastructure. 

So far, I've covered refineries, independent drillers and chemical producers.

I bet there's more out there. 

I bet the collision of the financialized world and the physical world is a stocked pond of opportunity right now.
